response: The global food system must produce 60% more food by 2050 to feed an expected 9.7 billion people, yet it is besieged by climate-driven yield losses, water scarcity (as seen in the rapidly depleting Ogallala Aquifer), and soil degradation on 33% of arable land. Agriculture itself generates about 25% of global greenhouse gas emissions and suffers from post-harvest losses that waste roughly one-third of all food. To meet these challenges, stakeholders are exploring AI-powered precision farming, urban vertical agriculture, alternative proteins (insects, cultured meat), drought-resistant GM crops and regenerative soil-building practices, alongside policy reforms in subsidies, trade and carbon pricing. Scaling these solutions while keeping food affordable and accessible to the poorest populations remains the key hurdle.
